scenarioName: Unwitnessed VF Cardiac Arrest at Home - Bystander CPR, Single Shock ROSC

scenarioStory.history: 55-year-old male went to his basement workshop after dinner to work on a project. Approximately 30 minutes later his wife heard a thud and a tool dropping. She went downstairs immediately and found him face-down on the workshop floor next to his workbench. She estimates he had been down for less than 5 minutes - she had heard him moving around just minutes before. She found no pulse and no respirations, called 911, and the dispatcher coached her through chest compressions and rescue breaths (30:2). She has been doing CPR for approximately 4 minutes when EMS arrives. Total estimated downtime is 8-10 minutes from collapse to EMS arrival. Pre-arrest, the patient had complained of intermittent indigestion-like discomfort over the past 3-4 days that he attributed to stress and dietary indiscretion.
scenarioStory.discussion: Recognize unwitnessed cardiac arrest with bystander CPR as still survivable when high-quality ALS care follows. Apply the cardiac arrest pit-crew model with defined roles and a code leader. Defibrillate as soon as the device is ready in unwitnessed VF arrests - no required delay for additional CPR cycles per protocol. Use the manufacturer's recommended (or highest) energy setting for biphasic defibrillation (200 J on Zoll X-Series). Resume compressions immediately after defibrillation without a pulse check for 2 minutes. Recognize ROSC by multiple methods: organized rhythm, palpable pulse, EtCO2 surge. Apply post-ROSC priorities: airway, ventilation 8-10/min, SpO2 94-98%, BP support, 12-lead, destination decision. Identify Anterior STEMI on 12-lead ECG (V1-V4 ST elevation) and activate the regional STEMI system. Avoid the two leading causes of post-ROSC re-arrest: hyperventilation and untreated hypotension. Plan for stair/awkward extrications with mechanical CPR device readiness in case of re-arrest. Manage shock-refractory VF with continued shocks, epinephrine, and amiodarone, and consider termination of resuscitation or ECMO referral as appropriate
scenarioStory.course: Arrival on Scene - Unwitnessed Arrest with Bystander CPR: EMS arrives at an unwitnessed cardiac arrest with bystander CPR in progress for approximately 6 minutes. Total estimated downtime is 8-10 minutes. Per protocol, for unwitnessed arrests it is reasonable to initiate (or continue) CPR while the defibrillator is being applied and to defibrillate as soon as the device is ready - there is no required delay for additional CPR cycles. The crew takes over compressions seamlessly, applies pads, identifies coarse VF, and prepares to defibrillate. → ROSC After First Shock - Initial Stabilization: After the single 200 J shock, compressions resumed for 2 minutes. At the next rhythm check the monitor shows an organized sinus rhythm and a palpable carotid pulse is detected. EtCO2 has surged from ~14 to 36 mmHg, consistent with sudden ROSC. The patient is comatose. The team transitions to the post-ROSC priorities: airway management, ventilation at 10/min (NOT faster), SpO2 94-98%, BP support, 12-lead, destination decision. Given the unwitnessed arrest, downtime ~8-10 min, and pre-arrest indigestion-like complaints, a STEMI is very plausible and a PCI-capable destination is indicated. → Stable Transport to PCI Center: Patient is en route to the regional PCI center with airway secured (i-gel), BVM at 10/min via mechanical ventilator with PEEP if available, EtCO2 38, SpO2 96% on titrated O2, SBP 108 with a 500 mL NS bolus running, no vasopressors needed at this time. Defibrillator pads remain in place. The crew vigilantly monitors for re-arrest, performs serial 12-leads, and provides early notification.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Found unresponsive in basement workshop, pulseless and apneic - cardiac arrest with bystander CPR in progress

scenarioEvents.name: Arrival on Scene - Unwitnessed Arrest with Bystander CPR
scenarioEvents.description: EMS arrives at an unwitnessed cardiac arrest with bystander CPR in progress for approximately 6 minutes. Total estimated downtime is 8-10 minutes. Per protocol, for unwitnessed arrests it is reasonable to initiate (or continue) CPR while the defibrillator is being applied and to defibrillate as soon as the device is ready - there is no required delay for additional CPR cycles. The crew takes over compressions seamlessly, applies pads, identifies coarse VF, and prepares to defibrillate.

scenarioEvents.name: ROSC After First Shock - Initial Stabilization
scenarioEvents.description: After the single 200 J shock, compressions resumed for 2 minutes. At the next rhythm check the monitor shows an organized sinus rhythm and a palpable carotid pulse is detected. EtCO2 has surged from ~14 to 36 mmHg, consistent with sudden ROSC. The patient is comatose. The team transitions to the post-ROSC priorities: airway management, ventilation at 10/min (NOT faster), SpO2 94-98%, BP support, 12-lead, destination decision. Given the unwitnessed arrest, downtime ~8-10 min, and pre-arrest indigestion-like complaints, a STEMI is very plausible and a PCI-capable destination is indicated.

scenarioEvents.name: Stable Transport to PCI Center
scenarioEvents.description: Patient is en route to the regional PCI center with airway secured (i-gel), BVM at 10/min via mechanical ventilator with PEEP if available, EtCO2 38, SpO2 96% on titrated O2, SBP 108 with a 500 mL NS bolus running, no vasopressors needed at this time. Defibrillator pads remain in place. The crew vigilantly monitors for re-arrest, performs serial 12-leads, and provides early notification.

scenarioEvents.name: Branch - Shock-Refractory VF
scenarioEvents.description: After the first shock, the patient remained in VF. After 2 minutes of CPR and a second shock at 200 J, VF persists. The team must escalate ACLS care: high-dose antiarrhythmic (Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO), continued CPR, continued shocks every 2 minutes, epinephrine 1 mg every 3-5 minutes, and a methodical search for reversible causes (H's and T's). Consideration of double-sequential defibrillation, ECMO, or termination of resuscitation may be appropriate at extended downtimes.

scenarioEvents.name: Branch - Re-Arrest from Hyperventilation / Untreated Hypotension
scenarioEvents.description: After ROSC, the team either hyperventilated, failed to support BP, or removed pads. The patient re-arrests in VF. Pads should still be on - shock immediately and resume CPR.

checklist.title: Take over compressions seamlessly from the fatiguing bystander
Re-establish high-quality CPR: 100-120/min, depth 2-2.4 inches, full recoil, minimal interruptions
Apply manual defibrillator pads in parallel with continuing compressions
Identify Coarse Ventricular Fibrillation on the monitor
Deliver first defibrillation at 200 J biphasic (Zoll highest setting)
Resume compressions immediately after the shock - no pulse check for 2 minutes
Establish IO access at the proximal humerus without interrupting compressions
Apply waveform capnography and target EtCO2 > 10 mmHg
Recognize ROSC: organized rhythm, palpable pulse, sudden EtCO2 surge
Manage post-ROSC airway with supraglottic airway and BVM at 10 breaths/min - do NOT hyperventilate
Target SpO2 94-98% post-ROSC - do NOT hyperoxygenate
Acquire post-ROSC 12-lead ECG and identify Anterior STEMI pattern
Transport to PCI/cath-capable facility with field STEMI activation
Leave defibrillator pads in place for the duration of post-ROSC care
Treat post-ROSC hypotension (SBP < 90) with NS bolus then push-dose epinephrine if needed
Provide clear, compassionate communication with the wife throughout the call
Designate a code leader and assign pit-crew roles before the first shock
Brief and direct the wife to a safe area without scolding her CPR effort
Establish a second IV in addition to the IO during transport
Place a supraglottic airway (i-gel) for the comatose post-ROSC patient lacking gag reflex
Plan stair extrication with a mechanical CPR device or LUCAS available in case of re-arrest
Provide structured SBAR handoff at the cath lab door with downtime, bystander CPR duration, time-to-first-shock, time of ROSC
Avoid hyperthermia post-ROSC by limiting warm blankets and bunting
